date: 2026-06-22
video_id: eA03X6-O8mQ
url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eA03X6-O8mQ
title: The Macro Minute: Will the disintegrating global economy continue to support global stock markets?

Every Monday at 13:42, it's your skipper here, Darius Dalebus, with our macro minute for Monday, June 22nd, 2026. As always, we'll start with the executive summary from today's lead-off morning note. So, let's dive right in. Today's key macro question is, will the disintegrating global economy continue to support the global stock market? The short answer is yes. The ongoing shift to a multipolar world represents a durable source of aggregate demand for AI, raw materials, and defense, among other things. Investors should continue to use the MAG 7 as a source of funds to capitalize undervalued winners in non-US stock markets. In terms of analytical nuance, China imposed export controls on two US rare earth producers, central to Washington's drive for Beijing-independent critical minerals supply chain. MP Materials and USA Rare Earth joined the list to, quote, "safeguard national security and interests," according to the Commerce Ministry. Uh China's Commerce Ministry. The curbs are mostly symbolic since both firms have already severed their reliance on Chinese equipment and material inflows. The action ends a months-long lull in incremental restrictions dating to last October's Trump-Xi summit. The Commerce Ministry cast it as retaliation for the Pentagon's recent listing of major Chinese firms as military backers. Recall that the Pentagon branded Alibaba, Baidu, and BYD as US national security threats for the aiding of the Chinese military. Currently, the global stock market is applying a near 0% probability that China wins the AI race, which we continue to view as being largely fabricated by the defense industrial complex for the purposes of expanding the wealth pump that Peter Turchin details throughout his seminal work on our disintegrating society. Per the April TIC data, stocks represent the overwhelming majority of foreign holding of foreign investors at 62%, double from the 31% ratio of 15 years ago. US stocks represent 48% of global equity market capitalization, despite the US only representing 25% of global GDP and 4% of the global population. Worse, US investors have a 63% allocation to US stocks, a massive overweight on top of the structural over allocation. Investors should expect this figure to decline materially over the coming decade. Refer to the fiscal policy and liquidity and positioning sections of our latest monthly macro scouting report for details regarding why. And finally, treasuries are now at an all-time low of 21% foreign investor holdings cut in nearly in half from their peak 38% ratio in July of 2012. Foreign investors currently own a record 31% of US stock market and only 33% of US Treasury market down from the peak of 53% 53% in July 2012. Foreign investors only own 23% of the Treasury bill market down from a peak of 50 50% in June of 2015. This represents a fantastic opportunity for financial repression, which is why Treasury-backed stablecoins have bipartisan support in DC. So, as always, we wrap up with a question from our community here in the 42 macro dashboard. This was titled "Reverse Portfolio Substitution Effect". Says, "High Skrpr, thanks for the very detailed analysis you presented to presented in the Around the Horn. I'm constantly impressed with the quality of your research and the way that you explain what's on your mind in layman's terms that we can understand and the consequences of that thinking on markets. I had a clarification question clarification question by reverse portfolio substitution effect. Do you mean that the Federal Reserve will implement bank deregulation so that the banks carry more treasuries on their balance sheet versus the Fed carrying those treasuries? So, uh Thanks for the kind feedback. So, just a quick clarification. So, no, we do not mean that is not what we were talking about with regards to the reverse portfolio substitution effect. I recall that during episodes of quantitative easing, Treasury bond yields tend to rise, I believe, on a median basis throughout the five episodes of quantitative easing we've had since the post-crisis era. They're up about 60 basis points. They're up about 20 basis points start the peak cuz obviously that the bond market has to discount the end of the program uh before the end of the program. And so, we've seen bond yields consistently rise despite these episodes of quantitative easing. And so, we surmise and other investors and and and academics have, you know, kind of call coined this uh, this dynamic called the portfolio substitution effect, whereby the central bank is essentially exchanging, uh, you know, it's doing an asset swap with the banking sector. It's giving the banking sector high-powered money in the form of bank reserves and removing the low-powered money in the form of treasuries and taking those and putting them on their own balance sheet. And so, that growth of high-powered money that is now in the private sector is both a signal and a conduit for banks and and other investors to lever up and extend further out on the risk curve. Now, if you put that in reverse, if the Fed starts to shrink its balance sheet, then you would ultimately have a reverse portfolio substitution effect, whereby the private sector, uh, you know, the commercial banking sector and and other private non-bank actors need to start taking down and then those treasuries, uh, and replacing and giving the Fed those bank reserves. And so, ultimately that's the signal that's what the signal and a conduit to force, uh, you know, the banks and and the private non-bank, uh, investors back in on the risk curve, and which ultimately cause bond yields to go down, uh, primarily but both as a function of the increa- increased demand, but also as a function of, uh, the uh, the reduction in nominal growth expectations that that would ultimately cause. Because again, you're pulling investor capital back in on the risk spectrum, which would ultimately lower nominal growth over time. So, that's what we mean by reverse portfolio substitution effect. Uh, we were talking about the the likelihood of substantial bank deregulation that we're going to see over the next few years to essentially pass the hot potato from the private non-bank sector, who shares about 58% of the marketable treasury market, back to the commercial banking sector, who shares now, uh, only 15% of the marketable treasury market, down from a peak of 33% in 2003. We think that process is, uh, this is going to be the the seminal work of the Warsh Fed in terms of pushing those treasuries, that hot potato, back into the commercial banking sector uh, in the coming, let's call it, two to two to four years. So that's our view on that and if that changes we'll let you know, but until then we'll have everyone have a wonderful day. I'll catch you back here tomorrow. Cheers.
